summary: Unable to connect to Mongo V2.0 when authentication is enabled
description: When authentication is enabled on a mongo v2.0 server, I am unable to connect to the server.  Perl errors out with "need to login" message.

summary: authenticated replicas don't work when you connect to a non-primary first or you haven't crafted your url correctly
description:
Here's a patch for another bug that manifests when you have replicas and use authentication.
I'm actually fixing two problems at the same time which I know is naughty but their symptoms are related.

First issue: if you don't specify a port in your host URL, it defaults to 27017 - as it should - but doesn't include that in the host attribute.  So when it comes to refresh the host server list as part of the master detection, it doesn't re-use the connection (because "host" != "host:27017") and creates a new one.  This on its own doesn't cause problems, but does create two connections when only one will do.

Second issue: when refreshing the host list as part of master determination, the code creates individual host connections without propagating the authentication info so these do not work.  Will only cause a problem in certain situations, but is not right.

summary: connect code fails to always correctly interpret the returned perl structure from 'authenticate'
description:
Whilst developing a fix for PERL-149, I discovered another problem.  In some cases, the perl SV returned from the 'authenticate' matches the test "SvTYPE(result) != SVt_RV" even though it does contain what we want (a pointer to a hash).  I believe this is because the test is naive, especially given the variation between Perl versions for SvTYPE (I'm using 5.12 BTW, but the types were revamped in 5.10 and 5.14 so there are some subtle differences across all the 'active' releases today).  The current test will pass for an IV object, but not for a PVIV object, which is perfectly valid.

Attached is a patch which does it in a better way, IMHO.  Instead of trying to work out the type of the object, it just checks for POK (valid string = failure message) or ROK (valid reference = hash we assume).

For good measure, I've also added in some previously missing variable de-references.  The patch is against git HEAD (effectively 0.45).

summary: Perl driver Collection::ensure_index ignores 'sparse' attribute
description: Attempting to create a 'sparse' index using the ensure_index routine results in an index that does not have the 'sparse' attribute

summary: [Perl Drivers] -- pm issue
description:
Hello,

I'am facing an issue while trying to use the perl driver.

I first installed the following :

{code} 8: MongoDB 0.45{code}

using the ppm (activestate). Nothing strange, just the standart use.

Then, whil trying to "use" my perl module (drivers), it crashes with the following output :

{code}install_driver(MongoDB) failed: Can't locate DBD/MongoDB.pm in @INC (@INC contai
ns: C:/Program Files/Perl64/site/lib C:/Program Files/Perl64/lib .) at (eval 14)
 line 3.
Perhaps the DBD::MongoDB perl module hasn't been fully installed,
or perhaps the capitalisation of 'MongoDB' isn't right.
Available drivers: CSV, DBM, ExampleP, File, Gofer, ODBC, Oracle, Pg, Proxy, SQL
ite, Sponge, mysql.
 at MonitoringDollarU.pl line 78{code}

What i see :

The "use" command, tries to use a pm located in @INC/DBD/MongoDB.pm. But to standart installation wants it to be installed in @inc (site/lib).

Any lead to share ?

Thanks in advance.

Anthony.

summary: find ignores field lists
description:
my @messages = $db->Mail->find({"queue" => $queue},{ live => 1})->all;

So all I want is the "live" field (for example), but when I look at messages, full documents exist:
priority owner app userid status _id %recips api stamp fromIP live queue templates expires

I even did a little collection and tried the one-liner from MongoDB::Examples
$db->users->find({}, {a => 1, b => 1});
And it contained all of the fields.

I'm more than happy to provide help as needed. I'll also dig into the driver later today and update this if anything is apparent. I swear this worked at one point (been using mongo for years) but perhaps I just wasn't playing close enough attention. This became apparent because I have some fields that contain arrays of ~1MB of data, and I've NOT wanted them in the results, but the queries were taking FOREVER to return, even after indexing... turns out that returning 200MB of JSON from California -> Vermont takes a while... Who knew? :)

summary: Chaining methods in Tailable workers not working
Unable to use utf8 strings as hash keys in Perl.
description:
Example of the failure: https://gist.github.com/1264725

This really should work, and it does work in the node.js native driver. After research, I see there is PERL-137 that fixes a segfault, but doesn't have proper behavior.

It's important to note that Perl itself supports unicode characters of higher byte orders to be hash keys, so this functionality should work.

Thanks,
-Jay

summary: Driver passes perl regex flags unknown to the db
description:
If you pass a regex like qr/blah/ux, the db logs

Wed May 23 14:50:16 warning: unknown regex flag:u
Wed May 23 14:50:16 warning: unknown regex flag:x

on every query. I am guessing the regex should get recompiled or stripped of the extended PCRE flags when converted into internal mongo format.

summary: utf8 checking in perl_mongo.c is too lax
description:
The function isUTF8 in perl_mongo.c is too lax when it comes to UTF-8 checking. It allows f.i. a byte sequence 0xc0 0xa0 to be inserted, while the UTF-8 standard defines that multibyte sequences cannot start with 0xc0 or 0xc1.  

I'm attaching a modified function with a few tests that (I hope!) accounts for all the specifications in Unicode 6.1, Chapter 3, Table 3.7 (pg 95 of http://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode6.1.0/ch03.pdf). Apologies for the somewhat clunky c though, especially the conversion from the char array to an int array, which works for me on a run of the mill Ubuntu 10.04, but hasn't been tested anywhere else. I'm hoping it'll be a no-op should char be unsigned...

Cheers.

summary: Adding the 'j' option for doing getLastError for safe writes
description:
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/pull/41

Just noticed there is no support for the 'j' option for safe writes, so I figured I would quickly add it.

Btw, to get the tests to run, I had to put the version number back in all the *.pm files. I didn't commit that because I saw you removed them and have been setting up stuff for the build so I didn't want to mess them up.

Also, the regex test also fails (at least for perl 5.12 and I bet for earlier versions as well) because the the bad flag just kills the compile. I can put that in an string eval or just submit a ticket later. 

Anyways, Thanks.

summary: Improving GridFS insert and delete speed
description:
https://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/pull/39

I noticed that for every insert and ever delete, we reinsert into system.indexes to create the two required GridFS indexes. I know have it where the indexes are created when the GridFS object is created. I also have added extra code so that if the database/collection/indexes are dropped after the GridFS object is created, it'll recreate them for the insert.

I also saw that the GridFS specification (http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/GridFS+Specification) requires the files_id/n index to have the unique option. So I added that in as well.

I also removed the line in the remove() function where it created the indexes because I didn't see why it was need. I tested out deleteing from GridFS with out the indexes and It worked just fine.

summary: Move build system to Dist::Zilla and Pod::Weaver
description: Get rid of Module::Install and move to dzil. Also set up PodWeaver.

summary: Perl driver tests will fail if you are running a master/slave or replica set
description:
t/connection.t does not localize its $conn variable when testing the host connection format for multiple hosts. This means if you happen to be running multiple mongod servers you will get a connection to a slave. This causes the test to die on the next test when it tries to insert into the [now] slave.

Fix is as in this commit: https://github.com/leejo/mongo-perl-driver/commit/0335fe7041eab38c348edae4a43296477ad757a0

summary: Ablitity to set "w" parameter to a string in Connection.pm
description:
We need the ability to set the "w" parameter to a string. This is becaues as of 2.0, we can tell it to replicate to the "majority" of the nodes. Plus with tagging and data center awareness, we can create new "getLastErrorModes" to say who we want the data to replicate. Here are the docs about it: 
http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/getLastError+Command#getLastErrorCommand-majority
http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Data+Center+Awareness

summary: AUTOLOAD must die
description:
The use of AUTOLOAD for collection names (and any other purpose I may not have noticed) is an interface bug.  Firstly it favors collection names that don't conflict with existing Colleciton methods.   Secondly it is fragile in the face of any new methods added to the Database class.  Thirdly it hides the accidental call of methods on the wrong object; if you accidentally call $database->get_dbase, well, you don't get an error, you get ->get_database('get_dbase').

Please remove this misfeature.

summary: slow connect
Prerequisite version of 'perl' is wrong
description:
Although the {{Makefile.PL}} says {{perl_version '5.8.4';}}, the actual code in {{MongoDB.pm}} says {{use v5.10.0;}}.

The practical effect of this is that someone like me who's installing on an old 5.8.8 perl will install 101 dependencies, and then half an hour later get an foreseeable failure. =(

summary: Perl driver speed
description:
Perl driver is very, very, very slow compared to PHP one...

Doing some tests with perl 5.10.1 and perl 5.12.3 show that PHP is between 2.8 to more than 3.2 times faster for basic insertions (without or with threads enabled).

In PHP case, mongod reach 100% and PHP stay around 57% CPU. 10M insertions done in ~100 seconds.

In Perl case, mongodb stay around 76% while Perl  is at 100% CPU ! 10M insertions done in ~280 seconds (threaded perl) or ~320 seconds (original, debian threaded perl).

I join the 2 sample scripts and a little patch that avoid calling sprintf() 12 times for each insertion. In the test case, 10% of exec time was spent in sprintf. With it we gain 10% of total time, not much, but better than nothing... :)
